sentences.text: Yeah, Dankowitz.
D-A-N-K-O-W-I-T-Z.
In Czestochowa, Poland.
I was born in May 24, 1920.
Now I'm 75.
Uh-huh.
In Europe?
Yeah, I have-- when I was born, my mother died.
I was 11 weeks old, and my mother passed away.
And my father didn't plan to remarry.
So they try to-- I have a older brother, three years older for me.
So they tried to give away to the grandparents.
But meanwhile, the-- one grandparents passed away [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a son-in-law.
And they got two children.
So they took it already, the two children.
They said they couldn't take a third one.
So they give me away for-- to raise me.
But it didn't help I got always sick.
So after two years, my father remarried.
And they took me home.
And I don't remember.
That's what I was told the story.
All right.
Well, let's just go back for a minute.
Because I want to get it clear.
After your mother died, they gave you away to be raised to whom?
I don't know to whom.
For money, for money, yeah.
Right, yeah.
To sleep over there and eat over there.
Yeah, and they paid.
I don't remember.
I was too small to say-- tell you.
He was with my grandparents, with the father's parents.
Right.
Did already have two children.
Yeah.
Right.
No.
Yeah, because I was-- I have a big belly.
And I couldn't walk.
And I got crooked legs, you know.
They call-- they called it some sickness.
By us they call it the [NON-ENGLISH].
Yeah.
The English Sickness, right.
So they took me-- my father remarried, and they took me home.
And I didn't remember anything.
But I remember when I was already five, and I started going to cheder.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So after then, I was seven.
I started going to cheder and an-- and a public school.
Should tell you the true, I didn't have special privileges, or special-- she didn't do nothing special wrong to me.
Because she has other two children right away.
So you know, if she give something, she give $0.10 more to the stepbrother, which I don't know.
Listen-- what you know you don't know.
But otherwise, I didn't saw any different.
And when I finished school, 14, they took me to machine-- start working.
Right.
My dad was a tailor, right.
With your dad, right.
Because the older brother work already with him.
And after then, they brought you in when you was already 13.
I already helped it out, too.
Because one time they needed work.
So they skipped today cheder, skipped today the school, you know, and helping out today.
Sew buttons, you know, clean up.
You know, we got our, like--
We made it and we selled it.
You know, I don't know how to explain to you, it's-- you know, like here is on Maxwell Street, you know.
You-- every-- every day you go out on the market and sell it.
And in the next city is a special market day.
So we took a-- hired a horse and buggy.
And we packed everything.
And we go over there.
You know, but three of us-- four-- my stepmother did.
Because we had always a, a, a helper in house to cook and to clean.
Mother was only in the business.
She only sells it in this way.
My father only was a cutter and helping out sewing.
Right.
Right.
After a time, we worked with three boys.
Because I got another stepbrother.
He was already working too.
Which baby?
Yeah, there was the girl--
We have a--
In the house, right.
So that's all--
Yeah.
A Polish girl.
She was 24 hours a day.
She get 10 zlotys a month, with food.
She was the cook.
She was the cleaner.
She was everything.
And then on Christmas you gave her a present.
This was the rules over there.
No, you see we have a three-- three rooms.
In Europe, is not like here.
In Europe we was used to be just four beds in one room.
And we was even not ashamed to sleep two people in one bed.
But the girl, she got-- the parents slept in the last room.
In the middle room, we have the-- like the [NON-ENGLISH], like the storage from the-- for the-- what we sew.
Yeah, but we pushed in a bed.
And the girl slept over there.
And we four brothers slept in the kitchen.
What?
In the bed.
There was four beds.
Right.
Was one bed was a table and a bed.
You ever heard of it, a table in a bed?
The table was-- you-- like this one.
After then, you pick up the deck and you choke it out this one.
[INAUDIBLE].
We didn't have mattresses.
We have straw and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a sack of straw.
No, no, not on top of the table.
Inside the table.
There was a box, you know.
And inside was the straw-- a sack with straw.
But when you through sleeping, you just push it together.
And you put the deck on top.
And you work on the table.
We pressed on the table-- everything.
It's not a clothing store.
The business was on the market.
The business was like-- it was like a store, but it's not a built store.
It was like a barrack.
Sewing machine was in the halls.
Right, we got four machines in the halls.
Yeah.
Czestochowa was about 45,000 Jews and 130,000 or 135,000 population.
Oh, a lot of thing.
It was the pogroms in Czestochowa, where they-- one-- a butcher [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] killed a Gentile.
I don't know why.
But there was a pogrom.
And we couldn't go out and-- you see, the Jews mostly live like in a ghetto.
You know, it was not a ghetto.
But it was-- this section lived the Jews.
So the Jews was-- we never was afraid of them.
But if they come and they-- in big-- in big quantity, big [INAUDIBLE].
Oh, maybe [INAUDIBLE] was 14 or 15.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The antisemitism was big, very big.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was-- you couldn't walk in every-- every street what you want to walk.
Like you want to go in park, you cannot go in park.
It was-- I don't know how they recognize a Jew right away.
Even if you didn't talk none, they recognize you as a Jew.
So I don't know.
Oh, very big.
Listen, whatever you're doing was no good.
Whatever you going to do is no good.
You see, there was-- the-- Poland has a law that you're not supposed to kill the Jew.
But if someone beats you up, it's OK.
You see, if they're staying here, and they wanted-- you come in and you want to buy something-- a pair of pants, or this one.
And he say, hey, don't buy by the Jew.
Well of course, who come?
The Gentiles, from this-- from the small towns, from the villages.
So he say, hey don't buy by the Jew.
You cannot say nothing.
Hey, go away.
He has a right to, to boycott your stuff.
According to the government, he has a right, and do something.
So always was-- So what we did, like, for example, we got that Gentile girl.
We took it already out a license on her name.
And we put her.
And after then, was one, a shoemaker-- he was a Gentile.
We build them up so he be a, a seller for this one.
We only charge him what the material cost and the work.
Any profit that makes after the work is his.
And, you know, Europe was a difference, because the work was cheap, but the food was cheap.
For example, if you buy a pair of pants for $2-- the whole pants-- and the material cost you a $1.50, so what cost you--
How you can sell it for $2 if the material cost $1.50?
And the lining-- and this will cost you $0.20.
So $0.30 for the work.
So you got $2.
You sell it.
You got $0.30.
You make 10 pairs a day, you got $3 a day.
And $3 a day was average what a factory man worked.
Because used-- they didn't hire Jews to a factory.
They didn't allow it.
Even they-- a Jew-- a Jew-- a Jew what has the factory, he wouldn't hire a Jew to the factory.
Because he was scared that they're going to put a fire on the factory or something.
He never hired a Jew to the factory, even if it was a Jewish factory.
So the Jewry must be self-- supporting themself.
It was a average what-- listen, $3 a day, you're not alone.
Your wife helped out, if you're married.
And you got children, like me-- we was four people working.
So we got $12 a day.
But it's the question, if you sell it so much, there's a reason you need the market.
So the market was that we got the two [NON-ENGLISH] this one, the shoemaker and the girl.
But after then, the girl was too big.
Because she say, I don't want anymore work in the house.
I want to be this, when she makes money over there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So somebody must take care on the house.
Nobody can take care on the house.
So-- and Jewish girls doesn't want to go.
So what did you do?
What did we do?
Nothing, what we do.
Once-- once a week we cooked up.
We left with the mother.
You helped out.
We did what we can.
The mother, the stepmother, in the night.
What can you do?
It was not-- it was not a easy life.
It's not America.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was very hard.
Yeah.
You see, in Europe, all Jews was observed.
There was not such a thing Conservative and this one.
If you're a Jew, you're a Jew.
You're Jewish because you're observing.
And if you-- unless you're a communist.
Because every Jew was an observer.
No, no.
Or you-- are a communist, which he doesn't believe.
Or you're a Jew.
We was-- I just told you.
We was praying.
Every day we put on the [INAUDIBLE].
Even we make it faster, and father said, hey, your head is not swell up.
You just put it down, already put it down?
You measured the head, if it's not swelling up?
You know.
We try to do what we can.
At home, yeah.
Oh, we didn't have time.
Because you see, we didn't use light.
To work, we only used daylight, the daylight.
We wake up [INAUDIBLE] in the summer, at 6:00.
And you work until 6:00, 7:30 in the night.
And we didn't even go to-- for-- supper was in the middle of the day.
There was not such as-- like here you go for supper out.
Supper-- and she brought the machine.
The girl brought the machine the food-- potatoes, with a borscht, with [NON-ENGLISH]-- hamburgers, you know.
You eat.
You finish.
You start work again.
Because the time was-- catch the light.
Because we don't work-- in this time was I think you supposed to pay for 1 kilowatt four, four hours work, for one kilowatt.
Was so high, the electricity, that we mostly didn't use electricity in, in [INAUDIBLE].
But we do the best what we can.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's it.
Oh, yeah.
All the holidays.
All holidays.
As a matter of fact, in our house really was a small-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what do you call it?
A small-- not a synagogue, a [NON-ENGLISH].
A shtiebel.
And they call it [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, when you're poor a girl, and if you cannot get married because of money, this, this one helped you out.
And only Friday they can get-- you can get married for them.
And they give you 25 zlotlys, you know.
And they prepare, give you a supper on Friday night.
And that's what you get.
But never-- they say never a Jew-- a Jewish wedding will fall apart because of money.
So this was their slogan.
And we was going.
There was a shul.
I remember when I was 16 years old, I was singing by a Hazzan He was the biggest in Poland, in Czestochowa.
He was taken away to Australia.
I get 25 zloty and two tickets for the parents.
For the high holidays.
Sure, you didn't know this?
In the synagogue.
See, it was only two synagogues in the city.
The rest was all-- oh, you got-- in every house, almost, a, a [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what a [NON-ENGLISH] mean?
A little this one, yeah.
But the synagogue-- there was the, the German synagogue.
And there was the state synagogue.
In the state synagogue, they used to give concert.
With the cantor-- our choir.
It was a choir.
In the choir.
No.
No.
I lost it.
I used to have a good voice.
No.
So that's where we was going.
But we never-- we didn't belong to the synagogue, because oh, it was very big-- big shots.
We belong to [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], you know-- to the little ones.
When my grandparents lived, we used to belong to [NON-ENGLISH].
Because the grandparents lived in the same house where [NON-ENGLISH] is.
So this reason, I was going even cheder over there, in the [NON-ENGLISH] because the grandparents took care of me-- I mean, the second grandparents.
The stepmother's parents, right.
Who remember cheder?
When you go-- start going to cheder, you think cheder, you think something else-- to play, this one.
Who remembers cheder?
I remember cheder when there used to be-- I don't know there was a law or not that said rabbi has a right to hit you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when I was in-- after my grandparents passed away-- so I was going [INAUDIBLE].
So he has this one.
And he hits you with a stick.
A ruler, right.
So he-- one guy, he hit him so hard.
And he starts-- this one.
And he was so nervous.
And he took the ink holder.
And he throw at him.
And he cut him out an eye.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was blind all the way.
The rabbi.
What can you did to, to the boy?
Nothing.
Oh, plenty times.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Plenty times.
He smoked cigarettes.
But he never got a match.
So he said, take my cigarette.
Go out and to the neighbor over and lighted up the cigarette.
So we go out.
And we light it up.
We bring him and say, rabbi, take fast, because it's burning already.
[LAUGHS] And we smoked it out there.
Right.
Me?
Everybody.
And how.
Everybody got hit.
Yeah, it was-- listen, we was no angels.
Yeah, I was observant.
Uh-huh.
Pesach.
All holidays was very good observed.
Because you see, we was not rich.
But we could afford it.
See, I don't remember when I was small.
But when we working already, four people working-- you can say five people, when mother works too-- so we could afford it.
We have food-- enough food.
We have every Saturday an apple, you know, for dessert.
And you take a nap.
Even the rabbi-- he comes-- he comes already Saturday.
He wants to have a free meal.
So he say to the father, I am going to show you what I teach your son today, this week.
And he takes out the [NON-ENGLISH].
And meanwhile, he wants to grab a free meal.
And what--
You know, kids are stupid.
I was like soccer-- socking playing-- soccer.
So Friday I told him, Sunday I'm going to be sick.
Because I want to go to the game.
But what a kid know?
He goes ahead and tells my father that I told him Sunday going to be sick.
And he ask me, why you going to be sick?
Because I want to go to the game.
You going to-- you're going to have a game.
So you didn't get to go to the game?
No.
Stupidity.
No, not a trouble maker.
I was not-- never a trouble maker.
Listen, I used to play soccer.
When I was 17, I used to be in the piazza, in the colosseum, and you play soccer.
And they charge already $0.50 to enter.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't get nothing for it.
But I play.
I liked to play soccer.
Yeah.
Like at here-- maybe more observant.
Maybe more observant.
And if you want to make a few extra pennies, you boil up a, a-- [INAUDIBLE] oil kettle, and put fire on the bottom.
And you have dishes-- come on, $0.10 cents a dip.
[LAUGHS] It was-- it was-- you know-- everything was--
Matzohs-- you don't buy matzohs by the pound.
You buy matzohs by-- there was a curb, you know.
And you must bring in your own [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what [NON-ENGLISH] mean?
No, I don't.
[NON-ENGLISH] mean where you're covering-- you're covering yourself a blanket.
And on top of the blanket, what are you putting on?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You cover the blanket.
A-- not a bedspread.
You put the blanket in.
Oh, I know what you mean.
But I forget the word too.
OK, you put the blanket in.
So you bring this one in.
Because if there rain, you going to [INAUDIBLE] the matzoh.
That's-- the matzoh's no-- no-- not kosher anymore.
Cannot be this way.
In the bakery.
In the bakery.
And you stay with the-- in the griddle.
You know, they make the holes.
You don't have to worry about this.
But they're just telling me that we're at the end of this first tape.
Oh, oh, oh, oh.
